title: Return to rural banking is a growth story for Westpac
full_text:
After years of shrinking their networks in rural Australia, banks are rediscovering the value of country communities and opening new branches.
Over the past 18 months, Westpac has been strengthening its rural network, re-establishing a presence in Moree in NSW, Leongatha in Victoria, Smithton in Tasmania and recently announced a return to Yass in NSW.
“To have the news that they were not only coming back to town, but occupying a main street empty shop, was phenomenal,” says Susannah Pearse, the mayor of Moree Plains Shire Council.
She says her community suffered a body blow when banks started shutting down their local branches a couple of years ago. “In a small town like ours, we have a lot of elderly residents who want to be able come down to the main street to do their chores for the day, and they want to go to a physical bank. It’s part of the liveability of the shire as well as the economy of the shire,” says Pearse.
Peta Ward, the national managing director of Westpac’s regional commercial and agribusiness unit, says when they looked closely at their network, they realised the decision to close the bank’s branch in Moree was a mistake.
“To be quite honest, we absolutely got this one wrong when we closed it a couple of years ago,” says Ward. “People still want that face-to-face, particularly if they are buying their first home or something significant, and we’re talking about the younger generation here: they’re as savvy as anything on apps and digital, but for those real-life big decisions, they actually want to be able to sit down and have that conversation with a human being,” she says.
Since 2017, banks have closed 37 per cent of their regional branches, and in places like Moree, the impact runs deeper than shops with nowhere to bank their takings, or first-home buyers wanting human reassurance before they commit.
Moree – 14,000 people spread out over 18,000sq km of fertile black soil – produced more than $1bn worth of agricultural crops last season, and is national leader in innovative farming techniques.
But even in a good year, farmers operate on margins of just 5 per cent: if their financial management isn’t as sophisticated and forward-looking as their farming, they risk losing money. “The majority of farmers rely fairly heavily on borrowings from banks to be able to plant their crops every year,” says Pearse, a farmer herself.
Farmers walk a financial tightrope every year, says Westpac’s Ward. “They’re dealing with tight margins, particularly at the moment when they’re facing increased interest rates and increased input costs like fertiliser and fuel,” she says.
“They’re having to become more and more sophisticated. We’re seeing clients looking at all sorts of different ways to manage the risk in their business, whether that’s through interest rate hedges or commodity hedges, or diesel hedges, or – if they are importing or exporting – currency hedges,” she says.
In some ways, farming can be a lonely business: unlike most companies with a comparable financial footprint, farmers have no committee to talk through strategy, or CFO to crunch the numbers, or a board to share the responsibility of big decisions, so Ward’s bankers serve as a crucial in-person sounding board for their agricultural clients.
“They absolutely want to be able to sit down and work through the cashflows,” she says. “They want to show you the property they’ve just bought, or the improvements they’re going to make or have made. They really do value having a person that they can sit down and talk it all through with.” She says there is a world of difference between day-to-day transaction banking and the sort of relationship Westpac builds with farmers: less bank manager and client than an active partnership that needs face time to thrive.
“If they’re just making an inquiry or something in their account, a digital assistant would be able to carry that out, but when it’s a big decision, then absolutely they want a trusted adviser or partner to be there at the table,” she says.
Banking for farmers “is about being able to really get to know your customer, or get in the ute and drive around the farm and look at the cattle or look at the crops and get that really deep understanding, not only of what they’re doing in the here-and-now, but also their dreams and aspirations,” she says. Since Westpac reopened its branch in Moree last October, it has delivered at least $200m in local agribusiness growth, underlining the key role that personal relationships play.
